brief_summary: Two-panel cross-section comparing the Alps SKCM Orange internal-snap mechanism against a generic MX-format tactile leaf-crossing mechanism. Left panel shows the Alps slider housing with a stepped body and a coral-highlighted internal snap point, labelled 'internal snap / no leaf crossing'. Right panel shows an MX stem with angled tactile legs approaching a horizontal metal leaf, with the leaf deflection in faint stroke, labelled 'leaf crossing / bump from contact'. The diagram makes the article's core claim visual: the Alps and MX mechanisms generate their tactile events through fundamentally different physical actions, and the Lanes attempts to approximate the Alps snap character within the MX footprint.
data_sources: gateron-lanes-tactile-deep-dive.mdx § 'What Alps SKCM Orange was' — 'a resonant slider body that physically interrupts its own travel path. The Alps tactile event is a snap, not a crossing. The slider compresses a small spring inside the housing, the spring releases, and the bump arrives as a discrete interruption'
gateron-lanes-tactile-deep-dive.mdx § 'What Alps SKCM Orange was' — 'The bump on an Alps SKCM Orange sits high on the travel, arrives quickly, and decays without drag'

gateron-lanes-tactile-deep-dive.mdx § 'How the Lanes draw from that heritage' — 'Gateron is not shipping an Alps-socket switch. The Lanes is an MX-compatible 5-pin switch — fits any MX PCB ... the fundamental mechanism is still a stem leg crossing a contact leaf.'
gateron-lanes-tactile-deep-dive.mdx § 'How the Lanes draw from that heritage' — 'the stem leg reaches the leaf, crosses it quickly, and releases with a snap-like immediacy'
